- It attributes the finding to an advocacy group: **“The sharp decline in coverage since 2011, recorded by the Media Matters for America advocacy group…”** (same URL)
- It provides a key numeric result: **“Media Matters found a 48% decline in climate-change coverage over a six-month period…”** (same URL)

**High-level summary:**
This Guardian piece reports on a *study/analysis* (by Media Matters for America) alleging that Reuters’ climate-change coverage dropped sharply after Paul Ingrassia (described in the story as a climate skeptic) became a senior editor. It quotes external voices (e.g., Ceres) arguing that climate change is a significant economic and financial risk and that reducing coverage is irresponsible.

The mandate requires that the scientific paper, government policy document, and organization position statement be found **by following links from the starting news article**.

=== https://web.archive.org/web/20171019221128/https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2013/jul/26/reuters-climate-change-scepticism-coverage ===
Share on Facebook
Share on Twitter
Share via Email
Share on LinkedIn

Share on WhatsApp
Share on Messenger
This article is
4 years old
Suzanne Goldenberg
, US environment correspondent
@suzyji
Friday 26 July 2013
15.31 EDT
First published on Friday 26 July 2013
15.31 EDT
Reuters' climate-change coverage fell by nearly 50% after a climate sceptic joined the news agency as a senior editor, a study has found.
The sharp decline in coverage since 2011,
recorded by the Media Matters for America advocacy group
, reinforces
charges from a former staffer
that Reuters cut back on climate stories under the influence of Paul Ingrassia, who is now the agency's managing editor.
Media Matters found a 48% decline in climate-change coverage over a six-month period, after Ingrassia joined the agency in 2011.
The New York Times and other news organisations have cut back on climate coverage, closing down blogs and redeploying correspondents, at times citing financial constraints. However, Bloomberg, Reuters' main competitor, has deepened its investment in climate change and sustainability coverage. The agency's founder, New York mayor Michael Bloomberg, has been a strong advocate for action on climate change.
Charges of an ideological component to Reuters' declining coverage – related to Ingrassia's personal doubts about established climate science – have sharpened concern in media and environmental as well as business circles, because of the agency's focus on financial news.
"It is just not responsible in our opinion to be cutting back on an issue that is having such a profound impact on every sector of the economy," said Mindy Lubber, who heads the Ceres sustainable business network, which represents companies and investors controlling some $11tn in assets. "This is a financial risk that needs to be looked at and addressed."
The
Climate Progress blog
has since criticised Reuters for injecting references in stories to fringe groups that reject established climate science, and represent barely 3% of scientists publishing on climate change.
The news agency did not respond specifically to the findings of a deep cut in climate-change coverage. But in an emailed statement, a spokesperson wrote:
"Reuters covers climate change closely both as a scientific and public-safety issue, as well as the impact of climate change on businesses, the economy and the markets. We have a dedicated staff, including a team of specialist reporters at Point Carbon and a columnist, who all generate significant coverage on the topic across our various platforms. We remain committed to providing fair and independent coverage of climate change that complies fully with our Trust Principles."
The scrutiny of Reuters' climate-change coverage began earlier this month when David Fogarty, the former Asia Climate Change Correspondent , wrote in a blog post that climate-change coverage had been dramatically cut back after Ingrassia's hire. Fogarty, a 20-year veteran at
Reuters
, covered climate change for four and a half years. But early last year it became increasingly difficult to get climate-change stories published. Editors suggested he pursue other stories. Then Fogarty described a conversation with Ingrassia, then deputy editor, at a social event.
"In April last year, Paul Ingrassia [then deputy editor-in-chief] and I met and had a chat at a company function. He told me he was a climate-change sceptic. Not a rabid sceptic, just someone who wanted to see more evidence mankind was changing the global climate.
"Progressively, getting any climate change-themed story published got harder. It was a lottery. Some desk editors happily subbed and pushed the button. Others agonised and asked a million questions. Debate on some story ideas generated endless bureaucracy by editors frightened to take a decision, reflecting a different type of climate within Reuters – the climate of fear.
"By mid-October, I was informed that climate change just wasn't a big story for the present, but that it would be if there was a significant shift in global policy, such as the US introducing an emissions cap-and-trade system. Very soon after that conversation I was told my climate change role was abolished."
Fogarty left the agency soon after.
Disclosure: the Guardian is a Reuters client.

details.intent: Find a starting news article about climate change with embedded/explicit links that can lead to (1) a scientific paper, (2) a government policy document, and (3) an organization's position statement. Output should include candidate article URLs and snippets indicating presence of such links.

From that article, follow links to find: (1) a scientific research paper or study, (2) a government policy document, and (3) an organization's position statement. For each, provide the URL, a summary of the content, and explain how it relates to the original article. Document your exploration path.

This article is
4 years old
Suzanne Goldenberg
, US environment correspondent
@suzyji
Friday 26 July 2013
15.31 EDT
First published on Friday 26 July 2013
15.31 EDT
Reuters' climate-change coverage fell by nearly 50% after a climate sceptic joined the news agency as a senior editor, a study has found.
The sharp decline in coverage since 2011,
recorded by the Media Matters for America advocacy group
, reinforces
charges from a former staffer
that Reuters cut back on climate stories under the influence of Paul Ingrassia, who is now the agency's managing editor.
Media Matters found a 48% decline in climate-change coverage over a six-month period, after Ingrassia joined the agency in 2011.
The New York Times and other news organisations have cut back on climate coverage, closing down blogs and redeploying correspondents, at times citing financial constraints. However, Bloomberg, Reuters' main competitor, has deepened its investment in climate change and sustainability coverage. The agency's founder, New York mayor Michael Bloomberg, has been a strong advocate for action on climate change.
Charges of an ideological component to Reuters' declining coverage – related to Ingrassia's personal doubts about established climate science – have sharpened concern in media and environmental as well as business circles, because of the agency's focus on financial news.
"It is just not responsible in our opinion to be cutting back on an issue that is having such a profound impact on every sector of the economy," said Mindy Lubber, who heads the Ceres sustainable business network, which represents companies and investors controlling some $11tn in assets. "This is a financial risk that needs to be looked at and addressed."
The
Climate Progress blog
has since criticised Reuters for injecting references in stories to fringe groups that reject established climate science, and represent barely 3% of scientists publishing on climate change.
The news agency did not respond specifically to the findings of a deep cut in climate-change coverage. But in an emailed statement, a spokesperson wrote:
"Reuters covers climate change closely both as a scientific and public-safety issue, as well as the impact of climate change on businesses, the economy and the markets. We have a dedicated staff, including a team of specialist reporters at Point Carbon and a columnist, who all generate significant coverage on the topic across our various platforms. We remain committed to providing fair and independent coverage of climate change that complies fully with our Trust Principles."
The scrutiny of Reuters' climate-change coverage began earlier this month when David Fogarty, the former Asia Climate Change Correspondent , wrote in a blog post that climate-change coverage had been dramatically cut back after Ingrassia's hire. Fogarty, a 20-year veteran at
Reuters
, covered climate change for four and a half years. But early last year it became increasingly difficult to get climate-change stories published. Editors suggested he pursue other stories. Then Fogarty described a conversation with Ingrassia, then deputy editor, at a social event.
"In April last year, Paul Ingrassia [then deputy editor-in-chief] and I met and had a chat at a company function. He told me he was a climate-change sceptic. Not a rabid sceptic, just someone who wanted to see more evidence mankind was changing the global climate.
"Progressively, getting any climate change-themed story published got harder. It was a lottery. Some desk editors happily subbed and pushed the button. Others agonised and asked a million questions. Debate on some story ideas generated endless bureaucracy by editors frightened to take a decision, reflecting a different type of climate within Reuters – the climate of fear.
"By mid-October, I was informed that climate change just wasn't a big story for the present, but that it would be if there was a significant shift in global policy, such as the US introducing an emissions cap-and-trade system. Very soon after that conversation I was told my climate change role was abolished."
Fogarty left the agency soon after.
Disclosure: the Guardian is a Reuters client.

=== https://web.archive.org/web/20171019221128/https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2013/jul/26/reuters-climate-change-scepticism-coverage ===
Share on Facebook
Share on Twitter
Share via Email
Share on LinkedIn
Share on Pinterest
Share on Google+
Share on WhatsApp
Share on Messenger
This article is
4 years old
Suzanne Goldenberg
, US environment correspondent
@suzyji
Friday 26 July 2013
15.31 EDT
First published on Friday 26 July 2013
15.31 EDT
Reuters' climate-change coverage fell by nearly 50% after a climate sceptic joined the news agency as a senior editor, a study has found.
The sharp decline in coverage since 2011,
recorded by the Media Matters for America advocacy group
, reinforces
charges from a former staffer
that Reuters cut back on climate stories under the influence of Paul Ingrassia, who is now the agency's managing editor.
Media Matters found a 48% decline in climate-change coverage over a six-month period, after Ingrassia joined the agency in 2011.
The New York Times and other news organisations have cut back on climate coverage, closing down blogs and redeploying correspondents, at times citing financial constraints. However, Bloomberg, Reuters' main competitor, has deepened its investment in climate change and sustainability coverage. The agency's founder, New York mayor Michael Bloomberg, has been a strong advocate for action on climate change.
Charges of an ideological component to Reuters' declining coverage – related to Ingrassia's personal doubts about established climate science – have sharpened concern in media and environmental as well as business circles, because of the agency's focus on financial news.
"It is just not responsible in our opinion to be cutting back on an issue that is having such a profound impact on every sector of the economy," said Mindy Lubber, who heads the Ceres sustainable business network, which represents companies and investors controlling some $11tn in assets. "This is a financial risk that needs to be looked at and addressed."
The
Climate Progress blog
has since criticised Reuters for injecting references in stories to fringe groups that reject established climate science, and represent barely 3% of scientists publishing on climate change.
The news agency did not respond specifically to the findings of a deep cut in climate-change coverage. But in an emailed statement, a spokesperson wrote:
"Reuters covers climate change closely both as a scientific and public-safety issue, as well as the impact of climate change on businesses, the economy and the markets. We have a dedicated staff, including a team of specialist reporters at Point Carbon and a columnist, who all generate significant coverage on the topic across our various platforms. We remain committed to providing fair and independent coverage of climate change that complies fully with our Trust Principles."
The scrutiny of Reuters' climate-change coverage began earlier this month when David Fogarty, the former Asia Climate Change Correspondent , wrote in a blog post that climate-change coverage had been dramatically cut back after Ingrassia's hire. Fogarty, a 20-year veteran at
Reuters
, covered climate change for four and a half years. But early last year it became increasingly difficult to get climate-change stories published. Editors suggested he pursue other stories. Then Fogarty described a conversation with Ingrassia, then deputy editor, at a social event.
"In April last year, Paul Ingrassia [then deputy editor-in-chief] and I met and had a chat at a company function. He told me he was a climate-change sceptic. Not a rabid sceptic, just someone who wanted to see more evidence mankind was changing the global climate.
"Progressively, getting any climate change-themed story published got harder. It was a lottery. Some desk editors happily subbed and pushed the button. Others agonised and asked a million questions. Debate on some story ideas generated endless bureaucracy by editors frightened to take a decision, reflecting a different type of climate within Reuters – the climate of fear.
"By mid-October, I was informed that climate change just wasn't a big story for the present, but that it would be if there was a significant shift in global policy, such as the US introducing an emissions cap-and-trade system. Very soon after that conversation I was told my climate change role was abolished."
Fogarty left the agency soon after.
Disclosure: the Guardian is a Reuters client.

Suzanne Goldenberg
, US environment correspondent
@suzyji
Friday 26 July 2013
15.31 EDT
First published on Friday 26 July 2013
15.31 EDT
Reuters' climate-change coverage fell by nearly 50% after a climate sceptic joined the news agency as a senior editor, a study has found.
The sharp decline in coverage since 2011,
recorded by the Media Matters for America advocacy group
, reinforces
charges from a former staffer
that Reuters cut back on climate stories under the influence of Paul Ingrassia, who is now the agency's managing editor.
Media Matters found a 48% decline in climate-change coverage over a six-month period, after Ingrassia joined the agency in 2011.
The New York Times and other news organisations have cut back on climate coverage, closing down blogs and redeploying correspondents, at times citing financial constraints. However, Bloomberg, Reuters' main competitor, has deepened its investment in climate change and sustainability coverage. The agency's founder, New York mayor Michael Bloomberg, has been a strong advocate for action on climate change.
Charges of an ideological component to Reuters' declining coverage – related to Ingrassia's personal doubts about established climate science – have sharpened concern in media and environmental as well as business circles, because of the agency's focus on financial news.
"It is just not responsible in our opinion to be cutting back on an issue that is having such a profound impact on every sector of the economy," said Mindy Lubber, who heads the Ceres sustainable business network, which represents companies and investors controlling some $11tn in assets. "This is a financial risk that needs to be looked at and addressed."
The
Climate Progress blog
has since criticised Reuters for injecting references in stories to fringe groups that reject established climate science, and represent barely 3% of scientists publishing on climate change.
The news agency did not respond specifically to the findings of a deep cut in climate-change coverage. But in an emailed statement, a spokesperson wrote:
"Reuters covers climate change closely both as a scientific and public-safety issue, as well as the impact of climate change on businesses, the economy and the markets. We have a dedicated staff, including a team of specialist reporters at Point Carbon and a columnist, who all generate significant coverage on the topic across our various platforms. We remain committed to providing fair and independent coverage of climate change that complies fully with our Trust Principles."
The scrutiny of Reuters' climate-change coverage began earlier this month when David Fogarty, the former Asia Climate Change Correspondent , wrote in a blog post that climate-change coverage had been dramatically cut back after Ingrassia's hire. Fogarty, a 20-year veteran at
Reuters
, covered climate change for four and a half years. But early last year it became increasingly difficult to get climate-change stories published. Editors suggested he pursue other stories. Then Fogarty described a conversation with Ingrassia, then deputy editor, at a social event.
"In April last year, Paul Ingrassia [then deputy editor-in-chief] and I met and had a chat at a company function. He told me he was a climate-change sceptic. Not a rabid sceptic, just someone who wanted to see more evidence mankind was changing the global climate.
"Progressively, getting any climate change-themed story published got harder. It was a lottery. Some desk editors happily subbed and pushed the button. Others agonised and asked a million questions. Debate on some story ideas generated endless bureaucracy by editors frightened to take a decision, reflecting a different type of climate within Reuters – the climate of fear.
"By mid-October, I was informed that climate change just wasn't a big story for the present, but that it would be if there was a significant shift in global policy, such as the US introducing an emissions cap-and-trade system. Very soon after that conversation I was told my climate change role was abolished."
Fogarty left the agency soon after.
Disclosure: the Guardian is a Reuters client.

details.goal: Start at a news article about climate change (find one via search). From that article, follow links to find: (1) a scientific research paper or study, (2) a government policy document, and (3) an organization's position statement. For each, provide the URL, a summary of the content, and explain how it relates to the original article. Document your exploration path.
details.original_goal: Start at a news article about climate change (find one via search). From that article, follow links to find: (1) a scientific research paper or study, (2) a government policy document, and (3) an organization's position statement. For each, provide the URL, a summary of the content, and explain how it relates to the original article. Document your exploration path.
details.goal_evaluation: Not achieved. The branch met the first subtask (identify a starting news article and enumerate its outbound links) — confirmed by the archived Guardian page and its captured link list — but failed to retrieve and open the three required document types (peer‑reviewed study, government policy document, organization position statement). Follow-up visits did not reach those target documents and instead returned the Wayback/Internet Archive root content, leaving the core deliverables (URLs + summaries + relation explanations) missing.
missing_requirements: A direct URL to a peer‑reviewed scientific paper or study linked from the news article and a short summary of that paper.
A direct URL to a government policy document (PDF or official page) linked from the news article and a short summary of that policy document.
A direct URL to an organization's formal position statement (NGO, industry group, or similar) linked from the news article and a short summary of that statement.
A documented exploration path showing which exact outbound link(s) from the starting article were followed to reach each of the three target documents (click sequence, timestamps, and node ids).

synthesized.summary: A suitable starting news article was located (an archived Guardian piece: "Reuters' climate-change coverage 'fell by nearly 50% with sceptic as editor'") and its outbound links were enumerated (160 links captured). The exploration recorded links to advocacy/analysis sites referenced in the article (Media Matters, ThinkProgress/Climate Progress) and to site navigation pages (Reuters, The Guardian categories). However, attempts to follow links to retrieve a peer‑reviewed scientific paper, a government policy document, and a formal organization position statement did not return those target documents — the follow-up visits instead landed on the Wayback Machine / Internet Archive root pages. Therefore the original objective (find and open one scientific paper, one government policy document, and one organization's position statement linked from the news article; provide URLs, summaries, and relation to the article) was not completed.
key_findings: Starting news article identified: The Guardian (archived) — "Reuters' climate‑change coverage 'fell by nearly 50% with sceptic as editor'". URL (archived): https://web.archive.org/web/20171019221128/https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2013/jul/26/reuters-climate-change-scepticism-coverage. Evidence: node_id 89b9011c-6291-4b8b-9ba9-bc01d7caeb79 (content_with_links and links list).

Outbound links enumerated from the archived article: 160 links captured, including explicit links to an item on Media Matters (http://mediamatters.org/… — archived link recorded) and to ThinkProgress/Climate Progress (thinkprogress.org/climate). Evidence: link lists and link_contexts in node_id 89b9011c-6291-4b8b-9ba9-bc01d7caeb79.

The article references/links mainly to media/advocacy pages and internal site navigation rather than obviously to a peer‑reviewed paper or a named government policy PDF. Evidence: inspection of the article's content_with_links shows Media Matters and Climate Progress links but no clear DOI/journal links or government policy PDFs in the captured link set (node_id 89b9011c-6291-4b8b-9ba9-bc01d7caeb79).

Attempts to follow links intended to retrieve (1) a scientific paper and (2) a government policy document resulted in visits to the Wayback Machine / Internet Archive root pages rather than the target documents. Evidence: node_id 7c00a837-e43f-47c2-9252-40a60d5b01ac and node_id fa0975f4-6764-4bae-9ea3-895200779a47 contain Wayback/Archive.org content rather than the requested documents.

No formal organization position statement (e.g., an NGO policy page or press statement PDF) was retrieved or summarized. The article does link to advocacy sites (Media Matters, Climate Progress), which may contain organizational commentary but were not followed to a formal position document in this run. Evidence: node_id 89b9011c-6291-4b8b-9ba9-bc01d7caeb79 (links) and absence of retrieved target docs in the follow-up nodes.
synthesized.goal_evaluation: Not achieved. The branch met the first subtask (identify a starting news article and enumerate its outbound links) — confirmed by the archived Guardian page and its captured link list — but failed to retrieve and open the three required document types (peer‑reviewed study, government policy document, organization position statement). Follow-up visits did not reach those target documents and instead returned the Wayback/Internet Archive root content, leaving the core deliverables (URLs + summaries + relation explanations) missing.
missing_requirements: A direct URL to a peer‑reviewed scientific paper or study linked from the news article and a short summary of that paper.
A direct URL to a government policy document (PDF or official page) linked from the news article and a short summary of that policy document.
A direct URL to an organization's formal position statement (NGO, industry group, or similar) linked from the news article and a short summary of that statement.
A documented exploration path showing which exact outbound link(s) from the starting article were followed to reach each of the three target documents (click sequence, timestamps, and node ids).
recommended_next_steps_to_close_gaps: Open the specific outbound links captured in the Guardian article that appear most likely to point to reports or statements — start with the recorded Media Matters URL (archived link at node_id 89b9011c-6291-4b8b-9ba9-bc01d7caeb79: http://mediamatters.org/blog/2013/07/23/reuters-climate-change-coverage-declined-signif/195015) and the ThinkProgress/Climate Progress link (thinkprogress.org/climate/). Follow them and capture the final URLs and content.

Search the Guardian article text for keywords that commonly indicate links to studies/reports (e.g., 'study', 'report', 'paper', 'IPCC', 'analysis', 'journal', 'DOI') and follow any associated hyperlinks directly rather than navigating via the Wayback root.

If the archived page lacks direct links to peer‑reviewed papers or government PDFs, pick an alternative news article that explicitly links to them (recommendations from initial search: Climate Action Tracker global update PDF — https://climateactiontracker.org/documents/1348/CAT_2025-11-13_GlobalUpdate_COP30.pdf — or government pages like DOE: https://www.energy.gov/topics/climate or NOAA https://www.climate.gov). Choose an article about an IPCC report, national climate plan, or COP decision; such pieces commonly link to (a) the IPCC report (scientific), (b) national NDCs / government policy PDFs, and (c) NGO statements.

When following links, record the exact click path and response (URL landed, HTTP status, page title) and capture the target document (or a reliable archived copy) so that a concise summary and relation to the original article can be produced with provenance (node ids and URLs).
provenance_map: Starting article and its outbound link enumeration: node_id 89b9011c-6291-4b8b-9ba9-bc01d7caeb79 — archived Guardian page and content_with_links (160 links captured).

Recorded specific outbound references (Media Matters, ThinkProgress): link entries and link_contexts inside node_id 89b9011c-6291-4b8b-9ba9-bc01d7caeb79 (e.g., https://web.archive.org/web/20171019221128/http://mediamatters.org/blog/2013/07/23/reuters-climate-change-coverage-declined-signif/195015 and https://web.archive.org/web/20171019221128/http://thinkprogress.org/climate/issue?mobile=nc).

Follow-up visits that returned Wayback / Internet Archive root instead of target docs: node_id 7c00a837-e43f-47c2-9252-40a60d5b01ac and node_id fa0975f4-6764-4bae-9ea3-895200779a47 (content shows Wayback/Archive.org landing pages).
